words: WILLIS ESTES: I made some notations here. I didn't know just what you would
ask, but I can tell you quite a little about it.
LAURA SCHRAGER: Okay.

speaker: WE
words: You know, after so many years something'll slip your mind, you don't

speaker: WE
words: remember exactly what all happened; and then sometimes something'll

speaker: WE
words: come back to you. Yeah, I took the examination here in Moscow, and there

speaker: WE
words: was eleven of us took it. I found out afterwards that I had the highest

speaker: WE
words: grade of any of them. The Pot Office Department notified the acting

speaker: WE
words: post master at Viola to write to the three highest and see if one of 'em

speaker: WE
words: would take the mail route out there. And as soon as I got the letter,

speaker: WE
words: why—see the Inland train was running from Moscow to Spokane, it was making

speaker: WE
words: three trips a day each way at that time--and I was out on the morning train

speaker: WE
words: and went to the Post Office, and went out with the carrier. He wasn't the

speaker: WE
words: regular carrier, he was the substitute that had been carrying the mail

speaker: WE
words: after the main carrier had moved. He left there; he didn't like it. I

speaker: WE
words: went around the route with him and when I come back I told the postmaster

speaker: WE
words: Id take it. He wanted to know when I'd be out and I said I'd be out a

speaker: WE
words: week from that day. He give me a week to do a little horse trading. The

speaker: WE
words: team I had wouldn't do for on the mail route, at that time. So I went out

speaker: WE
words: ahead of time, was there, and I started carrying on May the sixth, 1914.

speaker: LS
words: You were pretty young then.

speaker: WE
words: I was twenty. I would be twenty-one in the fall, but I was twenty then.

speaker: LS
words: What kind of--did you have to trade work horses in for a different kind

speaker: LS
words: of team?

speaker: WE
words: Yes, the team I had wouldn't do for on the road. I wanted to get travelers,

speaker: WE
words: smaller horse. Yeah, one that I had wouldn't have been any good on the road

speaker: WE
words: at all but she was a good work horse. And I knew horses, so I went out

speaker: WE
words: with one unbroke horse and one that was broke. And I soon broke the other

speaker: WE
words: one and used those two for quite a while.

speaker: LS
words: That one that wasn't broke—you mean it had never beeen in a harness

speaker: LS
words: before?

speaker: WE
words: No. It'd never had anything but a halter on it when I got it. Of course

speaker: WE
words: I was used to horses and used to riding, and a fellow wanted--I was looking

speaker: WE
words: for a horse and I heard he had one, and I went and talked with him and

speaker: WE
words: he said he had one and told me where it was. And I says, "Well, is it

speaker: WE
words: gentle enough I can get a hold of it?" And he says, "Yes. You can probably

speaker: WE
words: lead it in." So, I don't know, I took something out 'cause I knew the

speaker: WE
words: horse wouldn't like it, and I didn't think of taking the halter but I

speaker: WE
words: took a ridin' bridle. And before that horse knew it I had that ridin'

speaker: WE
words: bridle on 'im. And pretty soon I was on 'im. And I rode him to town,

speaker: WE
words: bareback. I never had any trouble with him. ^Course I knew I could ride

speaker: WE
words: before I got on him, but I didn't know--I didn't think he'd throw me

speaker: WE
words: (chuckles). Anyway I worked him for four or five years and then I got hold

speaker: WE
words: of another one. I traded horses different times. I had one horse that I

speaker: WE
words: traded for after, let's see, it was about three or four years, I traded for

speaker: WE
words: a full-blooded Hamiltonian. I worked him for fourteen years,and he was still

speaker: WE
words: a good horse at that time.

speaker: WE
words: When I started, that was the days of the two-cent letter mail, postage;

speaker: WE
words: one cent for the government postal card with a stamp one it. And I used one

speaker: WE
words: horse and the cart one day, and the other horse the next. Changed horses

speaker: WE
words: that way. That way each horse made three trips aweek. And the third day

speaker: WE
words: that I started, I went out and it was a fine looking day, same as the others

speaker: WE
words: had toeen. And about ten o'clock it clouded up fast and pretty soon it just

speaker: WE
words: poured rain. I really got soaked. So I got initiated (chuckles).

speaker: WE
words: I stayed with it anyway.

speaker: LS
words: You just had an open—?

speaker: WE
words: Open cart. Later on I got a little heavier axle cart. And I built a box

speaker: WE
words: and put a canvas on it that I could push ahead. And it would lay down over

speaker: WE
words: the dashboard and pull it back over my lap and the mail and have my feet

speaker: WE
words: and legs inside. And then if the horses wasn't afraid of an umbrella,

speaker: WE
words: I could carry an umbrella. But lots of times I had to jerk that umbrella

speaker: WE
words: down, and they'd get scared of it and start running. But then I soon got

speaker: WE
words: 'em used to it. If it wasn't too windy, why I could hold the umbrella and

speaker: WE
words: wouldn't get so wet. And then I soon got a raincoat and put it in the cart,

speaker: WE
words: strapped it on the underneath side of the seat, strapped it in there. The

speaker: WE
words: box that I had was about as long as this table and almost as wide, so I had

speaker: WE
words: lots of room at my feet and had a lot of room in back under the seat for

speaker: WE
words: packages.

speaker: WE
words: And the papers that came, I've forgot for sure which—there was two

speaker: WE
words: papers, part of the time, published here in Moscow. And one of 'em I

speaker: WE
words: think was what they call the Idaho Post. A fellow by£he name of Fields, I

speaker: WE
words: knew him. But whether that was right on the start or not I don't remember.

speaker: WE
words: Anyway, there were two papers, and I don't know which one was the first.

speaker: WE
words: Of course I carried the Daily Star Mirror and this Idaho Post. And one

speaker: WE
words: time there was one of 'em published that they called the News Review. And

speaker: WE
words: the Spokesman--there was a twice a week Spokesman-Review, delivered on

speaker: WE
words: Tuesdays and Fridays. And it was quite a while before |hey started the

speaker: WE
words: daily Spokesman, or at least it's quite a while before any of them on the

speaker: WE
words: route took the daily Spokesman.

speaker: LS
words: Did a lot of people take the newspapers?

speaker: WE
words: Quite a few of 'em did. But there was more took the twice a week than

speaker: WE
words: there was that took the daily, later on. There was-oh, must've been

speaker: WE
words: better than half more took the twice a week than they did when they started

speaker: WE
words: the daily because that's more expensive and they just dropped it. (Pause)

speaker: WE
words: Do you have any more questions before, or shall I just go ahead and tell

speaker: WE
words: yea-?

speaker: LS
words: Yeah, I'm interested to hear some of what you have down there. I have

speaker: LS
words: more questions, but...

speaker: WE
words: Well they were nearly all dirt roads. And sometimes the mud got awfully

speaker: WE
words: deep during the winter months and in the spring. And in the fall of the

speaker: WE
words: year I soon got a sleigh ready and put that box that I had on the cart on

speaker: WE
words: the sleigh, and I drove one horse to that sleigh. And then, after ten

speaker: WE
words: years I went out one morning,and it was thirty-two degrees below zero and

speaker: WE
words: facing a northeast wind. And I froze a spot on my cheek here and froze

speaker: WE
words: part of my toes. I didn't know they was froze but I got around over, oh,

speaker: WE
words: a little better'n halfway around, and one of the patrons come out, over

speaker: WE
words: in what they call the East Cove. And he said, "You know how cold it is?"

speaker: WE
words: And I said, "No, but I know it's cold." He says, "Ten degrees below

speaker: WE
words: zero," right then-the sun was shining bright, but there was just little

speaker: WE
words: sparkles of frost in the air. And I got home that night and I went in to

speaker: WE
words: the house and begin to thaw out, and I had to get outdoors-boy that hurt.

speaker: WE
words: Had to thaw out more gradual, outside. And then the nexipnorning it was

speaker: WE
words: still thirty-two degrees below zero, but the wind wasn't quite as strong.

speaker: WE
words: And, of course I went right on out anyway. And that spot on my face here,

speaker: WE
words: if it'd get down around pretty close to zero that'd turn blue yet from

speaker: WE
words: where I had it froze. And my toes that got froze-they swelled up and

speaker: WE
words: never did go down entirely, they get chilblains.

speaker: LS
words: Would you get stuck in the mud much?

speaker: WE
words: No, my horses I had was good and the rig was light, and I never got stuck

speaker: WE
words: in the mud. However there was once later on. . .Well after I froze part

speaker: WE
words: of my feet and the spot on my face, well I had a fellow over at Palouse

speaker: WE
words: build a metal cab ,and I put in on the buggy chassis, and I could close it

speaker: WE
words: all the way around. And then I had to--I drove two horses then—I had

speaker: WE
words: to get two more horses. And my salary when I started out was sixtyieight

speaker: WE
words: dollars a month, for me and the team. And then I finally got the front

speaker: WE
words: part of the bob. And then I could go with a team and take that nearly

speaker: WE
words: anyplace then. Anyplace they could go, why they could take that along

speaker: WE
words: with 'em because it was light and if the snow was on, why it'd pull easy.

speaker: WE
words: i But over in the Cove there'd be spots over there when the snow would melt

speaker: WE
words: off. And also around Viola, the wind'd blow in there and melt the snow.

speaker: WE
words: And I have walked sometimes two and a half, three miles a morning while

speaker: WE
words: the horse pulled the sleigh through the mud till we would get to where the

speaker: WE
words: snow was because I knew that the horse couldn't pull the rig through the

speaker: WE
words: deep snow on farther back. You see it was pretty hilly back there,and

speaker: WE
words: I crossed five ranges of mountains, and the snow got deep on them. And

speaker: WE
words: in the draws, why, some places I had to jump out of the rig and walk, rather

speaker: WE
words: than have the team pull me through the mud. But I did have the axle of

speaker: WE
words: the cart drag in the mud on one hill several times. So the mud was deep.

speaker: WE
words: 'Course I'd get out and walk up the hill, I'd walk out to the side.

speaker: LS
words: How long was thefoail route when you started?

speaker: WE
words: Nineteen and six-tenths miles. But I got some extensions on it, and before

speaker: WE
words: I transferred to Potlatch I was up to twenty-four miles. And the mail

speaker: WE
words: route that I got transferred to in Potlatch in '44, it was thirty-three miles,

speaker: WE
words: And it was an all year car route, so I sold my horses than and drove a car

speaker: WE
words: from that on, until I retired.

speaker: LS
words: If the weather was good, would it take you very long to deliver the mail?

speaker: WE
words: No, it wouldn't take very long. 'Gjarse I always give the horses plenty

speaker: WE
words: of time. But I'd leave about nine o'clock in the morning with the horses,

speaker: WE
words: and if the roads was good, why I'd be in by two-thirty or three. But in

speaker: WE
words: the wintertime when the snow was bad, why. . .

speaker: WE
words: There was one time, I don't remember what year it was, but there was

speaker: WE
words: one year that there was a week that the Inland couldn't get through from

speaker: WE
words: Moscow to Spokane nor Spokane to Moscow for a week. And, of course that

speaker: WE
words: cartied the mail in there. But there wasn't any mail for me to take out,

speaker: WE
words: only just what would be dropped in the post office at Viola or mailed

speaker: WE
words: from one rural route patron to another for me to deliver. But I'd go

speaker: WE
words: around and pick up what there was and left it there at the post office so

speaker: WE
words: it'd be delivered as soon as the train come through. And that one particular

speaker: WE
words: time—it got so bad that I couldn't get a team through, couldn't make it

speaker: WE
words: clear around in a day. But I could make it out as far as Flannigan Creek,

speaker: WE
words: then I'd leave my team there,and then I'd walk up around quite a loop and

speaker: WE
words: back to Flannigan Creek—take me an hour and a half, two hours, afoot,

speaker: WE
words: back to Flannigan Creek. And then I knew I couldn't go on from there

speaker: WE
words: either, so I'd drive back to Viola with it. And then the next day I'd

speaker: WE
words: go out on the back end of the route,and I'd get°as far as Bert Crooks'

speaker: WE
words: that was right at the foot of the big hill going from Viola to Potlatch

speaker: WE
words: and leave my team there in his barn. Then I'd walk clear over to

speaker: WE
words: Flannigan Creek and back, take the mail. I carried my snowshoes with me,

speaker: WE
words: and that's the way I did--you see I couldn't've walked through the snow

speaker: WE
words: without my snowshoes, and when I was doing that walking I was on snowshoes.

speaker: WE
words: One time I though, "Well I can save about three-quarters of a mile or

speaker: WE
words: maybe close to a mile by cutting back thrugh." See when I went out I went

speaker: WE
words: around the back way, I had to go out this way, and then when I come back

speaker: WE
words: I didn't have any mail so I cut through here. I was on snowshoes, and it

speaker: WE
words: was snowin' hard and blowin', and that hail and snow was just cuttin' on

speaker: WE
words: a fellow's face. Well I cut through there and through the timber, and that

speaker: WE
words: was fine for a while. And after I got over there quite a ways—the snow

speaker: WE
words: was deep too—I got in there quite a ways, and I broke the strap on one

speaker: WE
words: snowshoe. Well it'd been carrying up so good, and I thought, 'Well I can

speaker: WE
words: walk on it maybe anyway.1 So I put the snowshoes over my right—no, over

speaker: WE
words: my left shoulder, had 'em over my left shoulder, had a hold of 'em—arid

speaker: WE
words: the mail sack on my right. And all at once I broke through and went in

speaker: WE
words: clear up to here (chest level) and I couldn't walk agin' it.

speaker: LS
words: You went in all the way up to your chest?

speaker: WE
words: Yeah, yes. Just could get my arms out over the top of the snow. So I

speaker: WE
words: beat down some of it, enough so that the mail sack wouldn^t slip. See,

speaker: WE
words: when that mail sack, leather, hit that crusted snow, why it started—and

speaker: WE
words: I threw up my arm and caught it so it didn't go down the hill. And then I

speaker: WE
words: beat the snow down in a place there in a drift and set the mail sack there,

speaker: WE
words: and I tried to walk agin' that for a way but I couldn't walk agin' it. And

speaker: WE
words: there was brush underneath anyway. I didn't know just what to do for a

speaker: WE
words: t wile, and finally I though, 'Well I«ve got a leather shoestring m my

speaker: WE
words: pocket.And if I can jump out of here and light on one of these snowshoes,

speaker: WE
words: then I can fix that and put the snowshoes back on and go on.' So that's

speaker: WE
words: what I did. I was pretty active those days, I couldn't do that now (chuckles).

speaker: WE
words: I jumped up and lit stttin* down on one of them snowshoes, and it held me.

speaker: WE
words: So I sit right there and fixed that strap with that leather shoestring that

speaker: WE
words: I had; I always carried a leather shoestring in case the horse'd break some

speaker: WE
words: harness. They would, once in a while, get in a snowdirft dloundering

speaker: WE
words: around- why once in a while they'd break some of the harness. And I'd

speaker: WE
words: either patch it with a shoestring or baling wire and a pair of pliers

speaker: WE
words: or something, and go on. But anyway, that shoestring come in pretty

speaker: WE
words: handy. I kinda wondered for awhile just what I was gonna do, but it

speaker: WE
words: didn't worry me too much. I figured I could bore right back in the

speaker: WE
words: snow, get back in there quite aways and then poke just a hole, shut up

speaker: WE
words: where I went in, underneath the crust and fill it up all with just a

speaker: WE
words: breather. And go back in there, and I was dressed warm, and I'd've made

speaker: WE
words: it all right till morning. They'd've had trouble findin' me, because t

speaker: WE
words: was around three-thirty then.

speaker: LS
words: And you were way off the road.

speaker: WE
words: Yeah, I was clear off the road. There was one party knew that I had cut

speaker: WE
words: through their yard. She happened to be out there doin* chores, and I went

speaker: WE
words: through there and told her I was going right on out through there, save

speaker: WE
words: goin' around. Of course they had phones, but then I made it all right.

speaker: WE
words: But after that I didn't go through there anymore of course (chuckles).. Not

speaker: WE
words: on snowshoes I didn't.

speaker: LS
words: Did you have to use snowshoes a lot?

speaker: WE
words: Well, I didn't have to use them so awfully much, but I had 'em with me

speaker: WE
words: in case that I—you see, you never know when you might need them. And

speaker: WE
words: there was one big storm came. It came with the night—and a wet, heavy

speaker: WE
words: snow. And they had one place on Flannigan Crick that the black pines were

speaker: WE
words: thick on both sides of the road,and there wasn't harily any room to meet

speaker: WE
words: a team. If you met one, there there wasn't hardly room to pass. And it

speaker: WE
words: broke one tree down between four and five inches in diameter, and it laid

speaker: WE
words: right across that road, and I couldn't go around it. And I'd'a had to

speaker: WE
words: drove the team- I'd a had to unhook then and turn the rig around by hand—

speaker: WE
words: of course that was in the spring after the snow was down, and I was driving

speaker: WE
words: a wheeled rig. And I didn't have anything but a pocketknife with me. And

speaker: WE
words: I started whittling on that tree, and I cut it near enough that I could

speaker: WE
words: break it. And then I could handle the top part of it and move it enough

speaker: WE
words: so that I could drive the team through.

speaker: WE
words: And then in about the same place, later, the snow was fourteen, sixteen

speaker: WE
words: inches deep yet. And there come a rain and washed across the road there,

speaker: WE
words: and washed a ditch through the snow and then washed through the road. And

speaker: WE
words: it cut the road—it looked like it was a foot deep. And I had a span of

speaker: WE
words: mares, one four year old and one five year old. The four year old I had

speaker: WE
words: just broke her the spring before, and I knew I was gonna have trouble

speaker: WE
words: right then. I got out and I tromped some of the snow down, and I tried leadin'

speaker: WE
words: »em across. And I knew whenever one of 'em started—they'd jump. And I

speaker: WE
words: didn't know whether I could hold 'em or not. So I got back in the rig, and

speaker: WE
words: I had the front window tip—you've probably seen pictures of the mail wagons,

speaker: WE
words: haven't you? Well, anyway they have two little doors on the side, and then

speaker: WE
words: the window you can slide right on top of them doors, on either side. And

speaker: WE
words: then the front window is on a hinge, you can drop it down and there's a

speaker: WE
words: slot there for the lines to come through, or you can hook it up to the ceiling.

speaker: WE
words: Well I had it hooked up to the ceiling. And I got in and I tried to get

speaker: WE
words: »em to both jump at the same time. But one of 'em jumped and the other one

speaker: WE
words: backed up and broke the end of that tongue off, right back of the neck yoke.

speaker: WE
words: Broke it right square off. And then the other one jumped. Well, as the

speaker: WE
words: other one jumped it rammed that tongue into the opposite side of that ditch,

speaker: WE
words: and that rig just vaulted right up over that tongue. And when it come down,

speaker: WE
words: why it made such a noise, and of course the team was both a-runnin'

speaker: WE
words: their best (chuckles) by that time. And you couldn't guide it of course,

speaker: WE
words: because the tongue end was stickin' out between the back wheels of the

speaker: WE
words: buggy. I glanced back and saw what had happened. And the rig started for

speaker: WE
words: a big, black tamarack stump out to the side, and I knew they was gonna hit

speaker: WE
words: it. Well I grabbed pretty tight agin' the lines, I wasn't gonna let that

speaker: WE
words: team get away. And when they hit that stump, why they jerked me right outta

speaker: WE
words: that window—headfirst. They drug me for about two hundred yards before

speaker: WE
words: I could get 'em stopped. But they just mashed that right front wheel. And

speaker: WE
words: I went back and got the mail sack, put it on one and jumped on the other

speaker: WE
words: one and rode on down to the next neighbors there, and he happened to have a

speaker: WE
words: buggy. I borrowed his buggy and went on around with it.

speaker: WE
words: And then I had to—ther was a wagonmaker by the name of Anderson

speaker: WE
words: down in the blacksmith shop of Zumwalt and Collins--down about where the

speaker: WE
words: theater is now, on Main Street—at that time. And I called him and told

speaker: WE
words: him what I'd done, broke my doubletrees and singletrees and broke one

speaker: WE
words: wheel. And I went in then the next night—I took and used that feller's

speaker: WE
words: buggy the next day—and then the next night 1 went in there,and he had made

speaker: WE
words: me a pair of heavy doubletrees and singletrees. And I had told him that I

speaker: WE
words: thought I had another wheel 1hat would work. And I took it out and put it

speaker: WE
words: on and got by all right. That was one little experience that I had.

speaker: WE
words: Then another timfe1, it was whiletl was drivin' the sled. I got outta

speaker: WE
words: the rig and was walking, walkin' up a hill. And in places, let's see, the

speaker: WE
words: snow was on the leftthand side of the road, and I let the team walk along

speaker: WE
words: there. And I run over to the mailbox on the other side. And (?) had a

speaker: WE
words: dog out there, and I had a colt, a young three year old, and the three year

speaker: WE
words: old was on that same side I was in. Well, the dog run out a scairt that

speaker: WE
words: colt, and he took off, and I couldn't catch him. And I was dressed pretty

speaker: WE
words: heavy. And they ran fer—let's see, they made a mile west and then a quarter

speaker: WE
words: of a mile south and another mile west, and hit the highway down there by

speaker: WE
words: Bert Crooks'. And I ran into the house there where the dog was and there

speaker: WE
words: was nobody there. So I ran into the next mailbos, went in there and had him

speaker: WE
words: phone over to Bert Crooks tell ' im that my team was a-comin' in ahead of me.

speaker: WE
words: (laughter) And he said he got out there just in time. He said, "They come

speaker: WE
words: on down that hill there, comin' west and hit that highway.When they hit

speaker: WE
words: the highway that rig went"—you see they made a square turn see. And he

speaker: WE
words: said,"When that sled hit the highway, it went clear over and hit the snowbank

speaker: WE
words: on the other side but it didn't upset." And he caught 'em. Well, I wasn't

speaker: WE
words: very far behind 'em; the team was still pantin when I got there. But I was

speaker: WE
words: a-pantin' too. But they didn't lose but one package. I had left one door

speaker: WE
words: open, and when they jumped they jerked it when they jumped to start runnin'.

speaker: WE
words: They jerked it and that door went shut and the window went shut. So all the

speaker: WE
words: mail was in there.

speaker: LS
words: Did you have times when you did lose mail? You know, when something

speaker: LS
words: happened and it would get soaking wet or something like that?

speaker: WE
words: Oh, yeah. I've been wet clear through. And I've waded, got out and waded

speaker: WE
words: snow ahead of the horses.

speaker: WE
words: ELSIE ESTFS: He misunderstood you.

speaker: LS
words: That's all right. I was asking about the mail, whether the mail would get

speaker: LS
words: wet.

speaker: WE
words: No. No, I kept the mail dry.

speaker: LS
words: Not yourself, though (chuckles.)

speaker: WE
words: Yup, you see they had aleather mailbag, and it would turn water. I've had

speaker: WE
words: it wet all right enough, but it wouldn't wet through. It was kind of oiled

speaker: WE
words: I guess, 'cause it wouldn't wet through. It'd be kinda stiff after it

speaker: WE
words: dried off for a while, but then it'd soon be back again. No, 1 kept the

speaker: WE
words: mail good and dry. But I got out and broke trail ahead of the horses when—

speaker: WE
words: well on! was on the highway, after I had. . .

speaker: WE
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: WE
words: I started up there from Bert Qooks' and I got to the top of the hill. I

speaker: WE
words: couldn't go up the way I was supposed to. And I got to the top of the hill

speaker: WE

speaker: WE

speaker: WE
words: spots. But I don't know. I have wondered, I'll say it that way. I

speaker: WE

speaker: WE

words: Did you board in town when you first carried mail?

speaker: WE
words: At Viola? For about two weeks. I boarded there'with the acting postmaster,

speaker: WE
words: Mr. Oatney. And I rented a house and went to batching. I think it

speaker: WE
words: was just two weeks. I rented a house as quick as I could, and I had batched

speaker: WE
words: some before, anyway, and I batched then until I got married.

speaker: LS
words: Did you go to dances, parties, or anything?

speaker: WE
words: I didn't, no. No, I didn't go to the dances atall. No, I helped my folks,

speaker: WE
words: didn't have no money to go on (chuckles).

speaker: LS
words: There was something else that you told me about last time which was: the

speaker: LS
words: butchering that you did on the side?

speaker: WE
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: LS
words: YOu told some about how you learned from your father how to do it.

speaker: WE
words: Yeah, my father, he butchered quite a bit. And not only that, but he bought

speaker: WE
words: cattle for the markets here then, too. And if he didn't have anything else

speaker: WE
words: to do in the wintertime, why he'd go out and buy one on his own and butcher

speaker: WE
words: it and peddle it around. Of course you can't do that now, got to have it. . ,

speaker: WE
words: And I had twenty-one head there for avhile. And I'd raise 'em and whenVer

speaker: WE
words: I had one ready to butcher, why I'd dress it and take it in with the hide on

speaker: WE
words: and sell it in the market. And then anything that was prime, read good

speaker: WE
words: stuff, the markets didn't want to buy it unless that had some utility

speaker: WE
words: along with it. So a utility would be a cow or something that wasn't

speaker: WE
words: up in shape. So I'd buy something and tell 'em, "Now, I've got a No. 1 out

speaker: WE
words: there and also got you a utility," whatever it was, steer or cow or

speaker: WE
words: whatever it happened to be. I said, "I'll butcher it and bring it in

speaker: WE
words: at the same time." They'd say, "Okay, bring it in." So I'd take it in.

speaker: WE
words: And at one time, I had three hanging up out there oh the north end of a

speaker: WE
words: barn there at Viola, and there come a big snow right away after I butchered

speaker: WE
words: and couldn't get 'em out of there for a week. But they stayed froze, got

speaker: WE
words: froze and stayed froze, 'fore I could get in there to get 'em and get back

speaker: WE
words: out to the road, take 'em in. They finally opened up the roads around there,

speaker: WE
words: bulldozer or something, and I could get out with 'em.

speaker: LS
words: I remember something about—you got pretty good at guessing weighte, didn't

speaker: LS
words: you?

speaker: WE
words: Well, I sure was. Yeah. After I had been buying and selling to Mr. Leakey

speaker: WE
words: for a while there, why he asked me—this was during the war, before the

speaker: WE
words: war closed, in about '44 or '45- I think it was. Arid he wanted to know

speaker: WE
words: when we were going to bring in some more cattle—I met him on the street.

speaker: WE
words: And I said,"Well, I haven't anything to sell now, sold everything I got

speaker: WE
words: except what I want to keep for brooding purposes." And he says, "Well

speaker: WE
words: don't you know where there is something?" And I says, "Well I know where

speaker: WE
words: there's four steers, but I can't buy 'em." He says,"What d'ya mean you can't

speaker: WE
words: buy 'em?" And I says, "He wants too much money for 'em." And he quizzed me

speaker: WE
words: down, and he says, "What does he want for *em?" "He says he wants four

speaker: WE
words: hundred dollars for 'em." He says, "Well, what would thay dress out?" I

speaker: WE
words: says, "I don't know, Carl." "Oh," he says, "you know so close thet—(chuckles).

speaker: WE
words: And I had a book in my pocket and I pulled out that book and I had the

speaker: WE
words: weights down. I looke at 'em, and put down what each one of 'em, I

speaker: WE
words: figured,would dress out . And he added it up and he says, "It's 1650 pounds."

speaker: WE
words: And I said, "Yeah, that's what I would buy 'em at." He says,"Will they

speaker: WE
words: dres^out that much?" I says, "I don't know, but I would buy »em at that."

speaker: WE
words: "Well," he says, "you buy 'em at that-I'll buy 'em. You go get 'em and

speaker: WE
words: bring 'em in for tweaty dollars?" "Yeah," I says, "I'll go buy 'em and

speaker: WE
words: butcher 'em and bring 'em in for twenty dollars." And he says, "All right."

speaker: WE
words: So I did. And I brought *em in and they weighed 'em, and they weighed just

speaker: WE
words: twenty-four pounds more than what I told him they would. That was getting

speaker: WE
words: pretty close on four head, when you miss 'em twenty-four pounds. But I was

speaker: WE
words: guessing awful close on 'em. I could walk around the critter out there

speaker: WE
words: in the field and guess within twenty pounds right along what that one would

speaker: WE
words: dress out. But that was because I was looking 'em over and guessin' at

speaker: WE
words: 'em. Every time I'd guess at one, if I'd buy it, then I'd see it weighed.

speaker: WE
words: If I made any mistake on it, I'd know how much. And it takes the experience;

speaker: WE
words: I couldn't do it now. (Pause)

speaker: WE
words: Well, after I got transferred to Potlatch, I, as soon as I could, I

speaker: WE
words: bought one of them little Jeeps, four wheel drive, and got a cab built for

speaker: WE
words: it. Had it sent down to Spokane. And I transferred to Potlatch the first

speaker: WE
words: day of August '44. And I had, just before that, I knew that I couldn*t

speaker: WE
words: shoe my horses, as I said before, knew I couldn't shoe them very much longer.

speaker: WE
words: And my youngest brother was out there one day and he wanted to borrow my

speaker: WE
words: shoeing outfit, wanted to know if he could borrow 'em, he thought he could

speaker: WE
words: shoe his horse. I said, "You can have 'em under one condition." They had

speaker: WE
words: been wanting me to shoe different horses around, the neighbors around, wanted

speaker: WE
words: me to shoe their horses, and I had shod some of them. And I says, "Under

speaker: WE
words: one condition." He says, "What's that?" I says, "That is, if you don't

speaker: WE
words: bring 'em back." I said, "I'm through shoeing horses." (chuckles) So he

speaker: WE
words: took 'em. And they didn't anybody bother me after that—because I don't

speaker: WE
words: have my tool, I give 'em away.

speaker: WE
words: I had something else on my mind here a minute ago, but I lost it. Oh

speaker: WE
words: yeah, after I was carrying mail out of Potlatch, see I was still living in Viola,

speaker: WE
words: And I knew one night it was gonna storm. Told Elsie it was gonna storm

speaker: WE
words: and I said, "I'm gonna set that alarm for twelve o'clock." Went to bed

speaker: WE
words: about nine or nine-thirty. And I said, "It'll be storming before morning.

speaker: WE
words: It's gonna be bad."

speaker: LS
words: How could you tell?

speaker: WE
words: Oh, I can feel it, and see the way the weather is. I'll watch the weather

speaker: WE
words: and watch the sun and the moon and the stars, and I could purty near always

speaker: WE
words: tell when we'd get a storm. When you're out in every day you git so you

speaker: WE
words: can. And so I got up, I think it was at twelve, maybe twdve-thirty, that I

speaker: WE
words: set the clock for. Anyway, I got up and it had just started. Storm a-comin'.

speaker: WE
words: And I went right down to the garage and put chains on all four wheels on

speaker: WE
words: the Jeep, because I knew that it was already snowin', it was a-blowin'. And

speaker: WE
words: I made it over to Potlatch and got in there and went to the hotel, and

speaker: WE
words: therewasn't anybody around there. Nobody awake.no place, nobody in the

speaker: WE
words: office. So I thought well—I had taken a comfort along just in case I got

speaker: WE
words: stalled along the road so I could wrap up and tay 'till somebody plowed me

speaker: WE
words: out or something. And the garage there at Potlatch, where you go into the

speaker: WE
words: garage , it stands about like this, (illustrates with pieces of paper).

speaker: WE
words: Here's the highway road out here going up to Princeton, and here's where

speaker: WE
words: the door is going the garage. Well, when I got to Potlatch, then

speaker: WE
words: after I saw I couldn't get into the hotel without waking somebody up, and

speaker: WE
words: I didn't know which door to knock on, and I didn't want to bother 'em so I

speaker: WE
words: backed that Jeep down right in here, right up agin' this—and the storm was

speaker: WE
words: coming from the west, of course, and I was pretty well sheltered in there.

speaker: WE
words: And I laid down in the seat, put the cover over me, and went to sleep.

speaker: WE
words: And about one-thirty for some reason or other I woke up, and when I woke

speaker: WE
words: up there was somebody comin' he was, oh, about thirty, forty feet from

speaker: WE
words: me. And I slid the side window open on the Jeep—Charlie Olden, the

speaker: WE
words: night watchman. And he says, "What are you doin here this time of night?"

speaker: WE
words: And I says, "I thought I couldn't get here in the morning, Charlie. There

speaker: WE
words: was nobody over here up at the hotel, and I didn't get in." And he says,

speaker: WE
words: "Well, after about another hour and a half I'll be there, in such-and-such

speaker: WE
words: a room, you come over and I'll letcha in." And I said, "Well, I'm here now."

speaker: WE
words: And he said, "You get cold though." "No," I said, "I don't think so.

speaker: WE
words: Anyway, I'll drive up to the top of the hill over here but I won't be going

speaker: WE
words: on ver to Princeton. I'll just drive up to the top of the hill where I

speaker: WE
words: know I can turn around and come back." And he said, "Yeah, the top of that

speaker: WE
words: hill will be bad tomorrow." I drove up thereafter a while and had the

speaker: WE
words: heater going, and that warmed me up a litte. And drove back down and

speaker: WE
words: backed right in where I was before, and stayed there till morning. And then

speaker: WE
words: that night I couldn't get home. I started and I had made that day, making

speaker: WE
words: thirty-three or thirty-four miles, whichever it was, I think it was

speaker: WE
words: thirty-four then. Anyway, I started out and I got down and started up the

speaker: WE
words: hill, and all at once I thought,'Well now I wonder what's happened? I

speaker: WE
words: don't believe I'm moving but the car's still runnin'.' And I slid open that

speaker: WE
words: side window and the snow was right up half-way on that window on that

speaker: WE
words: Jeep and spinnin' all four wheel with chains on. So I just backed up and

speaker: WE
words: went back to Potlatch and got me a room, stayed overnight.

speaker: LS
words: Did that happen when you were using horses? That you'd get hung up and

speaker: LS
words: stay overnight somewhere?

speaker: WE
words: Just once that I had to stay overnight. One horse—well I wasn't much

speaker: WE
words: more'n half-way around at four o'clock, and the horse'd been wading snow till

speaker: WE
words: he was gettin' pretty tired, and D wasn't going to crowd him anymore.

speaker: WE
words: And there's that fellow by the name of Hanso, Adolph Hanson, and his

speaker: WE
words: brother lived there, Sig Hanson, right by the side of him. And Adolph

speaker: WE
words: come out and he says, "You can't make it on in tonight." And I said,

speaker: WE
words: "No, I can't." "Well," he says, "come°?n. We'll put you up for the

speaker: WE
words: night." So I went in and stayed overnight with him. And I didn't get

speaker: WE
words: in the next day till purty near twelve o'clock, so I didn't start out

speaker: WE
words: that day again. But then the next day I made it again.

speaker: LS
words: Would you sometimes get in really late at night?

speaker: WE
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, lots of times that I'd get up above Bert Crooks' there,

speaker: WE
words: a fellah by the name of Hale Price. And I'd get up to Hale Price,and he»d

speaker: WE
words: be out there, and if it was gettin* pretty dark, I*d say, "Hale,can I

speaker: WE
words: borrow some matches so I can read the names ontthe mail as I goon?" And

speaker: WE
words: hed go in and get me some matches. And then when Id get to a mailbox,

speaker: WE
words: I'd light a match and see, you know, to see the names on the mail. Put

speaker: WE
words: the mail in the bos and go on. Yeah, lots of nights I wouldn't get in till

speaker: WE
words: eight, nine o'clock. And I'd be wet clear to the hips. I'd walk and the

speaker: WE
words: overalls'd just, you know, they'd be froze and just pop and crack—the ice

speaker: WE
words: on 'em. Well I stayed with it, I kept in the hopes that I'd get transferred

speaker: WE
words: to a better route. When I took the route I thought, "Well, this isn't

speaker: WE
words: very much and it don't pay much, but maybe now that I've started,maybe I

speaker: WE
words: can get transferred." But I stayed with that for a little over thirty

speaker: WE
words: years before I could get a transfer.

speaker: LS
words: Did you keep applying for a transfer?

speaker: WE
words: Wheneve I knew of anything, or somebody was gonna retire, but seemed like

speaker: WE
words: they always had a substitute that the postmaster was in favor of, rather

speaker: WE
words: than to change to let anybody else in.

speaker: LS
words: They probably didn't want to let you go in Viola.

speaker: WE
words: Well, probably not, I don't know. I thought maybe they'd be glad to

speaker: WE
words: get rid of me, but I don't know (chuckles). No, I did a good job. One

speaker: WE
words: thing I did do, whenever anybody was sick, if they sent in for some medicine

speaker: WE
words: to come out by mail, I'd get it to 'em some way. Even if I had to walk

speaker: WE
words: in on my snowshoes and the mail sack on my back, I didn't leave the mail

speaker: WE
words: out of sight, no time. I'd leave the rig sometimes, quite a little ways,

speaker: WE
words: but then it was always vtere I could see it.

speaker: LS
words: Your story really—I mean a mailcarrier in those days, that really hard job,

speaker: LS
words: the It really took a lot just to deliver mail.

speaker: WE
words: Yeah that's right.

speaker: LS
words: I think of the slogan that the mailmen have: "Neither rain nor." Was

speaker: LS
words: that slogan around when you were working there?

speaker: WE
words: Oh, yes. I've seen that lots of times.

speaker: EE
words: There's another one you got; "Service with a smile."

speaker: WE
words: Yeah. "Sevice with a smile." But also: "Theinail must go through—rain,

speaker: WE
words: snow, or shine." I hired out to do the job, and I did it. Yeah, I've had

speaker: WE
words: lots of patrons tell me, "Well, better turn around and go back, you can*t

speaker: WE
words: make it." "Well, maybe I can't. I'm gonna try it." No, like I said, I

speaker: WE
words: hired out to do the jobs, and I always did the best that I could. That's

speaker: WE
words: all anybody could do. (Pause) Well the patrons, the appreciated it too,

speaker: WE
words: 'cause when I retired one fellow went around and made a purse for me and

speaker: WE
words: bought me a real nice pair of binoculars. (Pause) And I belonged to the

speaker: WE
words: Rural Letter Carriers Association for years. And I was Vice President for

speaker: WE
words: a long time, and then—I forgot what year it was—anyway I was elected

speaker: WE
words: President of the Rural Letter Carriers Association of Idaho. First elected

speaker: WE
words: for the District No. 5, that's from Grangeville north to the Canadian line.

speaker: WE
words: I was first Vice President and then President of the District for several

speaker: WE
words: years. And then at one of the meetings--

speaker: LS
words: What kind of association was that?

speaker: WE
words: It's just the rural letter carriers.

speaker: LS
words: Was it for the letter carriers to work out problems together?

speaker: WE
words: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: EE
words: It was affiliated with the National Association.

speaker: WE
words: It wasn't like these unions, but it was the carrier's association, they'd

speaker: WE
words: work out problems together. And then they'd have their National Concention,

speaker: WE
words: have their district meetings and state meetings. And generally at the state

speaker: WE
words: meeting they'd have a national officer there for any information, any new

speaker: WE
words: laws, and what-not. And, I'm still amember of it and get my paper.

speaker: LS
words: When did that start up?

speaker: WE
words: Well, I don't know, but as soon as I got a car, and that was in why I

speaker: WE
words: got notice that there was a meeting of the rural letter carriers of this

speaker: WE
words: district in Genesee. So I drove there and joined. And I've been a member

speaker: WE
words: ever since. Still am. And then. . .

speaker: LS
words: What were some of the issues that people would talk about? You know,

speaker: LS
words: what would they complain about the most, you know, in terms of their job?

speaker: WE
words: Well, there's so many different angles there,I couldn't start to tell

speaker: WE
words: you all of 'em.

speaker: EE
words: They were for higher salaries.

speaker: WE
words: They'd try to have mailbox improvememnt week, you know, and have everybody

speaker: WE
words: have their mailboxes up like they should be and the names on »em, in place

speaker: WE
words: of some of »em leaning over- not harig down. And also they have the rules

speaker: WE
words: about what kind of mailbok you can put up. Now there was a patron on the

speaker: WE
words: Viola mail route, they moved in there one day and stuck up afive gallon

speaker: WE
words: can out in front and put a letter in it. Well, I was afraid that letter'd

speaker: WE
words: blow away, so I took it out.Then the next day there's another letter in

speaker: WE
words: there, and I thought I'd better not start doing that anymore, so I quit.

speaker: WE
words: And then one day, a short time after that, why the lady came in to the

speaker: WE
words: post office. And the postmaster was out,and I was back in there putting

speaker: WE
words: up the mail, and she thought I was the postmaster, I guess. So she jumped

speaker: WE
words: onto the postmaster and was givin* the carrier the dickens, you know, for

speaker: WE
words: not picking up her mail (laughter). And there were three or four out

speaker: WE
words: in the front part—see, they had a little store there, too, at that time—

speaker: WE
words: and they was a-laughin'. She was just givin* that carrier a fit because he

speaker: WE
words: wouldn't take the mail out of the box (laughter). And finally I told her,

speaker: WE
words: I says, "Well, if you put up a regulation mailbox, the carrier will take it

speaker: WE
words: out of the mailbox and also bring your mail out. But he won't take the

speaker: WE
words: mail out and put it in that tin can you've got setting up out there.I'm

speaker: WE
words: the carrier!" And boy did she wilt, (chuckles)I told her it's agin* the

speaker: WE
words: law--postal laws and regulations. They have to have laws to govern every

speaker: WE
words: one of the carriers. And they try to keep 'em all somewhere near straight,

speaker: WE
words: like they should be and doing the business just like Uncle Sam wants it

speaker: WE
words: done. And if they get to losin* mail or not having the mail right or

speaker: WE
words: something, why the post office inspector will jump all over 'em, no doubt.

speaker: WE
words: I never had him jump onto me, but then I tried to be all right so he

speaker: WE
words: couldn't.

speaker: LS
words: Oh, did you ever switch to being paid by the hour, rather than being paid,

speaker: LS
words: you know, just for doing the job?

speaker: WE
words: No, no. I never did. Never had that chance.

speaker: EE
words: Well, there's none of 'em did. They were paid by the mile.

speaker: WE
words: Some of 'em do, yeah.

speaker: LS
words: Oh, you were paid ray the mile.

speaker: WE
words: Yeah, I was being paid by the mile. And they give you a regular time to

speaker: WE
words: make it in, if you can. Of course lots of time you can make it ahead of time,

speaker: WE
words: and sometimes you can't, but you had to fill out your trip report every day.

speaker: WE
words: Fill it out when you left and when you returned. And also, once ayear,

speaker: WE
words: why, you had to count the first, second, third and fourth class mail:

speaker: WE
words: how much you handled, how much you took out, how much you collected of

speaker: WE
words: each. And also the value of the stamps on all of 'em, both going out

speaker: WE
words: and coming in.

speaker: WE
words: (Portion on Rural Letter Carriers not transcribed)

speaker: LS
words: Did your pay increase very much in those. . .?

speaker: WE
words: Well, it did before I retired to be sure. Yeah I started, I don't remember

speaker: WE
words: now what I was gettin' finally, but then anyway, when I transferred to

speaker: WE
words: Potlatch why I got quite a bit more money than I did on the shorter route

speaker: WE
words: because they paid so much amile. That's one reason Iwas trying to get

speaker: WE
words: off of that route and get onto a longer mail route. I begun to think I

speaker: WE
words: was never gonna make it (chuckles).

speaker: LS
words: Well, it only took you thirty years.

speaker: WE
words: Yeah, little over thirty years. The sixth of May in 1914 until the last

speaker: WE
words: day of July—the first day of August I transferred to Potlatch. Then I

speaker: WE
words: carried until the last day of May, 1955. Then Iwas getting cripped up

speaker: WE
words: so bad with arthritis and hurtin' so bad, I just figured I'd better get

speaker: WE
words: out of it. So I retired. Then I went to work as night clerk in the Moscow

speaker: WE
words: Hutel. Worked there for fourteen months. Thought maybe if Igot in where

speaker: WE
words: I wasn't out in the cold, maybe that'd help my arthritis; it didn't, it

speaker: WE
words: just kept gettin worse. So they told me to go down to Arizona where it's

speaker: WE
words: hot and it'd help me. So in the fall of '56 I drove down there, went down

speaker: WE
words: there, and finally rented aplace. Iliked the winters so well down there,

speaker: WE
words: and before we left in the spring I bought. . ..
